title: Roman capitals, the inscriptional tradition
subtitle: The alphabet of the Trajan Column, written with a broad-edge nib
excerpt: Inscriptional Roman capitals are the letterforms cut into stone monuments in classical antiquity, the Trajan Column inscription of 113 AD is the canonical model. Written with a broad-edge nib at a variable angle (0° for thick downstrokes, ~30° for diagonals), they are the most geometrically exacting hand in the Western tradition.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on Roman capitals and the relationship between inscribed and written forms. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Johnston's analysis of the Trajan Column letters established the pen-written Roman capital as a legitimate calligraphic hand. The Trajan Column itself (113 AD) is in the public domain by age.

content.text: Roman capitals were carved into stone. The craftsmen first drew the letters with a brush, then followed the brush strokes with a chisel. The brush form translates straight to the pen form: thick verticals from a flat-held broad nib, thin diagonals from a nib turned to about 30°, and hairline
changes through the letters, unlike the fixed 30° of Foundational. That is what makes Roman capitals harder than the lower-case hands.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, size 0 (for large practice capitals)
Cartridge paper, 220 gsm, for finished work
Metal ruler and pencil for ruling

content.text: at 7 to 8 nib widths above the baseline. Roman capitals have no descenders; the cap line is the top of every letter.

The letters divide into width groups: narrow (B E F I J L P R S), medium (A C D G H K N O Q T U V X Y Z), and wide (M W). The M is approximately as wide as it is tall; the I is approximately one third as wide as the O. These proportions derive from the classical Roman system, not from a modern grid.

The O is the model letter for Roman capitals as the o is for Foundational. Hold the nib at 0° (edge flat, parallel to the baseline) at the widest points of the O; the angle twists to ~30° as the pen moves into the diagonal sections at the top and bottom. The result is maximum thickness at the sides (pure horizontal nib position) and minimum thickness at the top and bottom. Write the O in two strokes: left arc, then right arc, as in Foundational, but with the angle shift.

at the head and foot of each vertical stroke is formed by the nib corner. At the beginning of a downstroke: place the nib corner lightly on the paper at the cap line, draw a brief horizontal hairline left-to-right, then pivot to the vertical and draw the downstroke. At the end: the same hairline movement in reverse. The serif is the nib's thinnest possible mark, one corner only, barely touching.

is more complex than Foundational because the nib angle shifts within each letter. For the letter I: one stroke, drawn downward from cap line to baseline, with serif entries and exits at both ends. For the letter A: two diagonal strokes meeting at the apex (right diagonal first, left diagonal second), then a horizontal crossbar at the mid-height. The diagonal strokes are written at ~30° nib angle; the crossbar at a flatter angle for a medium-weight mark.

items.fix: The serif requires the nib on one corner only. Hold the pen so only the right corner of the nib touches the paper for the hairline. Load the nib lightly before serif work, a fully loaded nib floods.
items.cause: Too much ink in the nib, or the nib is being placed flat on the paper rather than on the corner.
items.symptom: The serifs are blobs rather than hairlines

items.fix: Practice the angle shift deliberately before attempting letters. Stroke from the 0° position to the 30° position on a ruled line, checking that the stroke width changes. The hand movement is a slight rotation of the wrist.
items.cause: The nib angle is not being consciously changed between the vertical strokes and the diagonal strokes.
items.symptom: The thick and thin strokes do not change predictably when the angle shifts
